event: Julius Caesar conquers the Belgae tribes, incorporating the region into the Roman world as part of Gaul.
Germanic peoples, including the Franks, cross the Rhine and settle the region as Roman authority collapses.
The Treaty of Verdun divides Charlemagne's empire, splitting the future Belgian lands between West and Middle Francia.
Flemish militias defeat French knights at the Battle of the Golden Spurs near Kortrijk.
The Flemish counties pass to the Dukes of Burgundy, beginning Burgundian unification of the Low Countries.
Mary of Burgundy marries Maximilian of Habsburg, bringing the Low Countries under Habsburg rule.
Charles V, the future Holy Roman Emperor and ruler of a vast empire, is born in Ghent.
The Eighty Years' War begins as the Low Countries revolt against Spanish Habsburg rule.
Spanish forces recapture Antwerp, cementing the split between the Protestant north and the Catholic south.
The Peace of Westphalia confirms the Southern Netherlands as Spanish territory while closing the Scheldt to trade.
The Treaty of Utrecht transfers the Southern Netherlands from Spain to Austrian Habsburg rule.
The Brabant Revolution erupts against Austrian rule, briefly proclaiming the United Belgian States.
Revolutionary France annexes the Austrian Netherlands and the Prince-Bishopric of Liege.
The Congress of Vienna unites the Belgian provinces with the Netherlands under King William I.
The Belgian Revolution breaks out in Brussels, leading to the proclamation of an independent Belgium.
Leopold I becomes the first King of the Belgians and a liberal national constitution is adopted.
Continental Europe's first passenger railway opens between Brussels and Mechelen.
The Treaty of London formally recognizes Belgian independence and guarantees the nation's neutrality.
Leopold II ascends the throne, beginning a long reign marked by industrialization and colonial ambition.
Leopold II establishes the Congo Free State as his personal colonial possession.
Belgium introduces universal male suffrage, though weighted by a plural voting system.
Amid international outcry over abuses, the Belgian state annexes the Congo Free State as the Belgian Congo.
Germany invades neutral Belgium at the start of World War I, triggering Britain's entry into the war.
Belgium is liberated as World War I ends, and full universal male suffrage soon follows.
Belgium and Luxembourg form an economic union, an early step toward regional integration.
Nazi Germany invades Belgium, which capitulates after eighteen days and endures occupation.
Allied forces liberate most of Belgium, though the Battle of the Bulge soon erupts in the Ardennes.
Belgium becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Belgian women gain full voting rights in national elections.
Belgium becomes a founding member of NATO, which later moves its headquarters to Brussels.
King Leopold III abdicates amid the Royal Question controversy and is succeeded by his son Baudouin.
Belgium signs the Treaty of Rome as a founding member of the European Economic Community.
Brussels hosts the World's Fair, Expo 58, and unveils the iconic Atomium.
The Belgian Congo gains independence amid turmoil that triggers the Congo Crisis.
Belgium fixes the linguistic border between its Dutch- and French-speaking regions.
The first state reform begins transforming Belgium into a federal state with cultural communities.
The Heysel Stadium disaster in Brussels kills 39 football fans before a European Cup final.
Constitutional reform formally establishes Belgium as a federal state, and King Baudouin dies, succeeded by Albert II.
The Maastricht Treaty takes effect, with Brussels serving as a principal seat of the new European Union.
Belgium adopts the euro as its currency and legalizes euthanasia under strict conditions.
Belgium becomes the second country in the world to legalize same-sex marriage.
Belgium sets a world record after 541 days without an elected government amid political deadlock.
King Albert II abdicates and Philippe becomes King of the Belgians.
Coordinated terrorist bombings strike Brussels Airport and a metro station, killing 32 people.
